name: Simple two-table join
query:
SELECT o.id, o.total_amount, c.email
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.status = 'pending'
explainOutput:
-> Nested loop inner join  (cost=4111 rows=8334) (actual time=0.488..9.05 rows=8334 loops=1)
    -> Index lookup on o using idx_status (status='pending')  (cost=1194 rows=8334) (actual time=0.478..4.02 rows=8334 loops=1)
    -> Single-row index lookup on c using PRIMARY (id=o.customer_id)  (cost=0.25 rows=1) (actual time=502e-6..518e-6 rows=1 loops=8334)

name: Three-table join with aggregation
query:
SELECT c.email, COUNT(o.id) AS order_count, SUM(o.total_amount) AS lifetime_value
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
JOIN payments p ON p.order_id = o.id
WHERE p.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY c.email
explainOutput:
-> Table scan on <temporary>  (actual time=119..121 rows=27215 loops=1)
    -> Aggregate using temporary table  (actual time=119..119 rows=27215 loops=1)
        -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=32278 rows=39837) (actual time=1.93..89.3 rows=50000 loops=1)
            -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=18335 rows=39837) (actual time=1.92..59.9 rows=50000 loops=1)
                -> Index lookup on p using idx_status (status='completed')  (cost=4392 rows=39837) (actual time=1.91..24.8 rows=50000 loops=1)
                -> Single-row index lookup on o using PRIMARY (id=p.order_id)  (cost=0.25 rows=1) (actual time=603e-6..619e-6 rows=1 loops=50000)
            -> Single-row index lookup on c using PRIMARY (id=o.customer_id)  (cost=0.25 rows=1) (actual time=485e-6..502e-6 rows=1 loops=50000)

name: Join with filter on both tables
query:
SELECT o.id, oi.product_id, oi.quantity, oi.line_total
FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id
WHERE o.status = 'shipped' AND oi.quantity > 3

    -> Filter: (oi.quantity > 3)  (cost=0.674 rows=0.899) (actual time=0.00237..0.00267 rows=0.99 loops=25000)
        -> Index lookup on oi using idx_order (order_id=o.id)  (cost=0.674 rows=2.7) (actual time=0.00218..0.00249 rows=2.49 loops=25000)

indexRecommendations.reason: `idx_status` reads 24.7K rows — a composite covering index (`quantity`, `id`, `status`) would be more selective and avoid table lookups
indexRecommendations.ddl:
-- Composite covering index: current index + WHERE + JOIN + aggregate columns
ALTER TABLE `orders` ADD INDEX `idx_orders_composite` (`quantity`, `id`, `status`);

  `name` varchar(100) COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci NOT NULL,
  `parent_id` bigint DEFAULT NULL,
  `slug` varchar(100) COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci NOT NULL,
  `sort_order` int NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  UNIQUE KEY `idx_slug` (`slug`),
  KEY `idx_parent` (`parent_id`)
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=256 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4 COLLATE=utf8mb4_unicode_ci

CREATE TABLE `orders` (
  `id` bigint NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `customer_id` bigint NOT NULL,
  `status` enum('pending','processing','shipped','delivered','cancelled','refunded') COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',
  `total_amount` decimal(12,2) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0.00',
  `discount_amount` decimal(10,2) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0.00',
  `shipping_amount` decimal(8,2) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0.00',
  `payment_method` enum('card','paypal','bank_transfer','crypto') COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci NOT NULL DEFAULT 'card',
  `shipping_country` char(2) COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci DEFAULT NULL,
  `notes` text COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci,
  `ordered_at` datetime NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  `shipped_at` datetime DEFAULT NULL,
  `delivered_at` datetime DEFAULT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  KEY `idx_customer` (`customer_id`),
  KEY `idx_status` (`status`),
  KEY `idx_ordered_at` (`ordered_at`)
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=100001 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4 COLLATE=utf8mb4_unicode_ci

CREATE TABLE `order_items` (
  `id` bigint NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `order_id` bigint NOT NULL,
  `product_id` bigint NOT NULL,
  `quantity` int NOT NULL DEFAULT '1',
  `unit_price` decimal(10,2) NOT NULL,
  `discount_pct` decimal(5,2) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0.00',
  `line_total` decimal(12,2) NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  KEY `idx_order` (`order_id`),
  KEY `idx_product` (`product_id`)
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=250001 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4 COLLATE=utf8mb4_unicode_ci

CREATE TABLE `payments` (
  `id` bigint NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `order_id` bigint NOT NULL,
  `amount` decimal(12,2) NOT NULL,
  `currency` char(3) COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci NOT NULL DEFAULT 'USD',
  `status` enum('pending','completed','failed','refunded') COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',
  `provider` varchar(50) COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci DEFAULT NULL,
  `provider_ref` varchar(100) COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci DEFAULT NULL,
  `paid_at` datetime DEFAULT NULL,
  `created_at` datetime NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  KEY `idx_order` (`order_id`),
  KEY `idx_status` (`status`)
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=80001 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4 COLLATE=utf8mb4_unicode_ci

CREATE TABLE `reviews` (
  `id` bigint NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `product_id` bigint NOT NULL,
  `customer_id` bigint NOT NULL,
  `rating` tinyint NOT NULL,
  `title` varchar(200) COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci DEFAULT NULL,
  `body` text COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci,
  `is_verified` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',

  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  KEY `idx_product` (`product_id`),
  KEY `idx_customer` (`customer_id`),
  KEY `idx_rating` (`rating`)
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=60001 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4 COLLATE=utf8mb4_unicode_ci

CREATE TABLE `inventory_log` (
  `id` bigint NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `product_id` bigint NOT NULL,
  `warehouse_id` bigint NOT NULL,

  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),

CREATE TABLE `warehouses` (

  `city` varchar(100) COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci DEFAULT NULL,

  `is_active` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '1',
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`)

CREATE TABLE `shipping_events` (
  `id` bigint NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,

issues.title: Row estimate mismatch on `c`
issues.description: Estimated 60,230 rows, actually 4,755 (0.1x fewer).

issues.title: High cost node: 100% of total
issues.description: This operation accounts for 100% of the total query cost. It is the primary bottleneck.

issues.title: Covering index on `c`
issues.description: All required columns are available in the index — MySQL doesn't need to read the actual table rows.

issues.title: Antijoin optimization
issues.description: MySQL converted a NOT IN / NOT EXISTS subquery into an efficient antijoin. This avoids re-executing the subquery for each outer row.

name: Multi-join order pipeline
query:
SELECT o.id, c.email, p.amount, se.event_type, se.event_at
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN payments p ON p.order_id = o.id
JOIN shipping_events se ON se.order_id = o.id
WHERE o.status = 'shipped'
AND p.status = 'completed'
ORDER BY se.event_at DESC
LIMIT 100
explainOutput:
-> Limit: 100 row(s)  (actual time=119..119 rows=100 loops=1)
    -> Sort: se.event_at DESC, limit input to 100 row(s) per chunk  (actual time=119..119 rows=100 loops=1)
        -> Stream results  (cost=35823 rows=32843) (actual time=2.16..117 rows=19216 loops=1)
            -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=35823 rows=32843) (actual time=2.15..112 rows=19216 loops=1)
                -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=24328 rows=17123) (actual time=2.08..70.3 rows=12556 loops=1)
                    -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=18335 rows=17123) (actual time=2.08..61.4 rows=12556 loops=1)
                        -> Index lookup on p using idx_status (status='completed')  (cost=4392 rows=39837) (actual time=2.05..20 rows=50000 loops=1)
                        -> Filter: (o.`status` = 'shipped')  (cost=0.25 rows=0.43) (actual time=749e-6..761e-6 rows=0.251 loops=50000)
                            -> Single-row index lookup on o using PRIMARY (id=p.order_id)  (cost=0.25 rows=1) (actual time=628e-6..644e-6 rows=1 loops=50000)
                    -> Single-row index lookup on c using PRIMARY (id=o.customer_id)  (cost=0.25 rows=1) (actual time=611e-6..627e-6 rows=1 loops=12556)
                -> Index lookup on se using idx_order (order_id=p.order_id)  (cost=0.48 rows=1.92) (actual time=0.0029..0.00323 rows=1.53 loops=12556)

name: GROUP BY without composite index
query:
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt, SUM(total_amount) AS total
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'delivered'
GROUP BY customer_id
explainOutput:
-> Table scan on <temporary>  (actual time=18.3..19.5 rows=24162 loops=1)
    -> Aggregate using temporary table  (actual time=18.3..18.3 rows=24162 loops=1)
        -> Index lookup on orders using idx_status (status='delivered')  (cost=5355 rows=49940) (actual time=1.54..11.3 rows=33332 loops=1)

name: GROUP BY with HAVING
query:
SELECT product_id, AVG(rating) AS avg_rating, COUNT(*) AS review_count
FROM reviews
GROUP BY product_id
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5 AND AVG(rating) < 3
explainOutput:
-> Filter: ((count(0) >= 5) and (avg(reviews.rating) < 3))  (cost=12050 rows=9708) (actual time=4.6..35.9 rows=3247 loops=1)
    -> Group aggregate: avg(reviews.rating), count(0), avg(reviews.rating), count(0)  (cost=12050 rows=9708) (actual time=4.59..35.1 rows=9970 loops=1)
        -> Index scan on reviews using idx_product  (cost=6085 rows=59651) (actual time=4.59..30.4 rows=60000 loops=1)

name: GROUP BY with ORDER BY different column
query:
SELECT brand, SUM(price * stock_qty) AS inventory_value
FROM products
WHERE is_active = 1
GROUP BY brand
ORDER BY inventory_value DESC
explainOutput:
-> Sort: inventory_value DESC  (actual time=5.5..5.5 rows=12 loops=1)
    -> Stream results  (cost=1086 rows=12) (actual time=0.626..5.49 rows=12 loops=1)
        -> Group aggregate: sum((products.price * products.stock_qty))  (cost=1086 rows=12) (actual time=0.623..5.48 rows=12 loops=1)
            -> Filter: (products.is_active = 1)  (cost=989 rows=965) (actual time=0.109..4.57 rows=9500 loops=1)
                -> Index scan on products using idx_brand  (cost=989 rows=9652) (actual time=0.107..4.15 rows=10000 loops=1)

queryHints.title: GROUP BY without ORDER BY (MySQL 8.4+)
queryHints.suggestion: If you need sorted output, always add an explicit ORDER BY clause.

name: ORDER BY on non-indexed column
query:
SELECT id, email, created_at
FROM customers
WHERE status = 'active'
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 20
explainOutput:
-> Limit: 20 row(s)  (cost=2759 rows=20) (actual time=13.6..13.6 rows=20 loops=1)
    -> Sort: customers.created_at DESC, limit input to 20 row(s) per chunk  (cost=2759 rows=24938) (actual time=13.6..13.6 rows=20 loops=1)
        -> Index lookup on customers using idx_status (status='active')  (cost=2759 rows=24938) (actual time=0.0626..11.7 rows=35000 loops=1)

            -> Filter: (products.is_active = 1)  (cost=989 rows=965) (actual time=0.0298..1.88 rows=9500 loops=1)
                -> Table scan on products  (cost=989 rows=9652) (actual time=0.0282..1.45 rows=10000 loops=1)

issues.title: Full table scan on `orders`
issues.description: Scanning 30,000 rows. The optimizer cannot use an index to filter rows, so it reads every row in the table.

issues.title: Filesort on 30,000 rows
issues.description: MySQL must sort 30,000 rows in memory or on disk. This is expensive for large result sets.

issues.title: High cost node: 100% of total
issues.description: This operation accounts for 100% of the total query cost. It is the primary bottleneck.

indexRecommendations.reason: Covering index for `orders` — filter on `shipping_country` + covers `status` without table lookup
indexRecommendations.ddl:
-- Covering index: filter cols first, then GROUP BY/ORDER BY, then SELECT cols
ALTER TABLE `orders` ADD INDEX `idx_orders_covering` (`shipping_country`, `status`);

simulatedImpact.summary: eliminates full scan, index-only scan, eliminates filesort

changes.explanation: Index `shipping_country, status` enables ref access instead of scanning all 30.0K rows

changes.before: Index lookup + table data read (random I/O)
changes.explanation: All needed columns (`shipping_country`, `status`) are in the index — MySQL never touches the table data

changes.after: Index delivers rows in sorted order
changes.explanation: Index column order (`shipping_country` → `status`) matches WHERE + ORDER BY — MySQL skips the sort step

schemaIssues.issue: `orders`.`shipping_country` is nullable but used in WHERE filter. NULL values can cause unexpected results (e.g., NOT IN with NULLs returns no rows).

name: ORDER BY with expression
query:
SELECT id, total_amount, discount_amount, (total_amount - discount_amount) AS net
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'delivered'
ORDER BY (total_amount - discount_amount) DESC
LIMIT 50
explainOutput:
-> Limit: 50 row(s)  (cost=5355 rows=50) (actual time=13.7..13.7 rows=50 loops=1)
    -> Sort: net DESC, limit input to 50 row(s) per chunk  (cost=5355 rows=49940) (actual time=13.7..13.7 rows=50 loops=1)
        -> Index lookup on orders using idx_status (status='delivered')  (cost=5355 rows=49940) (actual time=1.39..10.6 rows=33332 loops=1)

name: Correlated subquery in WHERE
query:
SELECT c.id, c.email
FROM customers c
WHERE (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id = c.id) > 5
explainOutput:
-> Filter: ((select #2) > 5)  (cost=5076 rows=49876) (actual time=0.114..64 rows=832 loops=1)
    -> Covering index scan on c using idx_email  (cost=5076 rows=49876) (actual time=0.0609..5.02 rows=50000 loops=1)
    -> Select #2 (subquery in condition; dependent)
        -> Aggregate: count(0)  (cost=0.734 rows=1) (actual time=999e-6..0.00101 rows=1 loops=50000)
            -> Covering index lookup on o using idx_customer (customer_id=c.id)  (cost=0.492 rows=2.42) (actual time=681e-6..847e-6 rows=2 loops=50000)

queryHints.title: Scalar subquery in SELECT — rewrite as LEFT JOIN
queryHints.suggestion: Rewrite as: SELECT t1.col, COALESCE(SUM(t2.amount), 0) FROM t1 LEFT JOIN t2 ON t2.fk = t1.id GROUP BY t1.id, t1.col. The LEFT JOIN + COALESCE ensures rows with no match show 0 instead of NULL.

name: Subquery with IN
query:
SELECT id, name, price
FROM products
WHERE id IN (SELECT product_id FROM order_items WHERE quantity >= 5)
explainOutput:
-> Nested loop inner join  (cost=80.2e+6 rows=802e+6) (actual time=35.1..39.5 rows=9929 loops=1)
    -> Table scan on products  (cost=989 rows=9652) (actual time=0.0309..1.26 rows=10000 loops=1)
    -> Single-row index lookup on <subquery2> using <auto_distinct_key> (product_id=products.id)  (cost=33497..33497 rows=1) (actual time=0.00369..0.00373 rows=0.993 loops=10000)
        -> Materialize with deduplication  (cost=33497..33497 rows=83075) (actual time=35.1..35.1 rows=9929 loops=1)
            -> Filter: (order_items.quantity >= 5)  (cost=25190 rows=83075) (actual time=0.283..30 rows=49822 loops=1)
                -> Table scan on order_items  (cost=25190 rows=249250) (actual time=0.282..21.3 rows=250000 loops=1)

queryHints.title: Scalar subquery in SELECT — rewrite as LEFT JOIN
queryHints.suggestion: Rewrite as: SELECT t1.col, COALESCE(SUM(t2.amount), 0) FROM t1 LEFT JOIN t2 ON t2.fk = t1.id GROUP BY t1.id, t1.col. The LEFT JOIN + COALESCE ensures rows with no match show 0 instead of NULL.

name: NOT IN subquery (anti-join pattern)
query:
SELECT id, email FROM customers
WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT customer_id FROM orders WHERE status = 'delivered')
explainOutput:
-> Nested loop antijoin  (cost=249e+6 rows=2.49e+9) (actual time=15.4..32.7 rows=25838 loops=1)
    -> Covering index scan on customers using idx_email  (cost=5076 rows=49876) (actual time=0.025..4.46 rows=50000 loops=1)
    -> Single-row index lookup on <subquery2> using <auto_distinct_key> (customer_id=customers.id)  (cost=10349..10349 rows=1) (actual time=499e-6..499e-6 rows=0.483 loops=50000)
        -> Materialize with deduplication  (cost=10349..10349 rows=49940) (actual time=15.4..15.4 rows=24162 loops=1)
            -> Filter: (orders.customer_id is not null)  (cost=5355 rows=49940) (actual time=1.55..11.5 rows=33332 loops=1)
                -> Index lookup on orders using idx_status (status='delivered')  (cost=5355 rows=49940) (actual time=1.55..10.3 rows=33332 loops=1)

issues.title: Large materialized subquery (25,838 rows)
issues.description: The subquery result is materialized into a temporary table with 25,838 rows. This happens once, but large materializations consume memory and may spill to disk.

issues.title: Covering index scan on `customers`

issues.title: Antijoin optimization

name: Scalar subquery in SELECT
query:
SELECT o.id, o.total_amount,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM order_items oi WHERE oi.order_id = o.id) AS item_count
FROM orders o
WHERE o.status = 'pending'
LIMIT 100
explainOutput:
-> Limit: 100 row(s)  (cost=1194 rows=100) (actual time=0.345..0.352 rows=100 loops=1)
    -> Index lookup on o using idx_status (status='pending')  (cost=1194 rows=8334) (actual time=0.345..0.348 rows=100 loops=1)
-> Select #2 (subquery in projection; dependent)
    -> Aggregate: count(0)  (cost=0.79 rows=1) (actual time=0.00128..0.0013 rows=1 loops=100)
        -> Covering index lookup on oi using idx_order (order_id=o.id)  (cost=0.521 rows=2.7) (actual time=872e-6..0.0011 rows=2.65 loops=100)

name: EXISTS subquery
query:
SELECT p.id, p.name
FROM products p
WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM reviews r WHERE r.product_id = p.id AND r.rating = 5)
explainOutput:
-> Nested loop inner join  (cost=20.9e+6 rows=209e+6) (actual time=5.6..9.45 rows=6960 loops=1)
    -> Table scan on p  (cost=989 rows=9652) (actual time=0.029..1.15 rows=10000 loops=1)
    -> Single-row index lookup on <subquery2> using <auto_distinct_key> (product_id=p.id)  (cost=4687..4687 rows=1) (actual time=724e-6..756e-6 rows=0.696 loops=10000)
        -> Materialize with deduplication  (cost=4687..4687 rows=21632) (actual time=5.56..5.56 rows=6960 loops=1)
            -> Index lookup on r using idx_rating (rating=5)  (cost=2524 rows=21632) (actual time=1.05..4.44 rows=11937 loops=1)

issues.title: Full table scan on `p`
issues.description: Scanning 6,960 rows. The optimizer cannot use an index to filter rows, so it reads every row in the table.

issues.title: High cost node: 100% of total
issues.description: This operation accounts for 100% of the total query cost. It is the primary bottleneck.

issues.title: Large materialized subquery (6,960 rows)
issues.description: The subquery result is materialized into a temporary table with 6,960 rows. This happens once, but large materializations consume memory and may spill to disk.

indexRecommendations.reason: Full table scan on `p` (6,960 rows) with filter on `rating`

changes.explanation: Index `rating` enables ref access instead of scanning all 7.0K rows

queryHints.title: Scalar subquery in SELECT — rewrite as LEFT JOIN
queryHints.suggestion: Rewrite as: SELECT t1.col, COALESCE(SUM(t2.amount), 0) FROM t1 LEFT JOIN t2 ON t2.fk = t1.id GROUP BY t1.id, t1.col. The LEFT JOIN + COALESCE ensures rows with no match show 0 instead of NULL.

name: Join needing covering index
query:
SELECT o.id, o.ordered_at, SUM(oi.line_total) AS order_total
FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id
WHERE o.status = 'processing'
GROUP BY o.id, o.ordered_at
explainOutput:
-> Table scan on <temporary>  (actual time=64.8..65.5 rows=15324 loops=1)
    -> Aggregate using temporary table  (actual time=64.8..64.8 rows=15324 loops=1)
        -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=31531 rows=80527) (actual time=0.804..51.5 rows=41862 loops=1)
            -> Index lookup on o using idx_status (status='processing')  (cost=3347 rows=29858) (actual time=0.796..7.28 rows=16668 loops=1)
            -> Index lookup on oi using idx_order (order_id=o.id)  (cost=0.674 rows=2.7) (actual time=0.00216..0.0025 rows=2.51 loops=16668)

issues.title: Temporary table with 15,324 rows
issues.description: MySQL creates a temporary table to process this operation. For large result sets, this may spill to disk.

indexRecommendations.reason: Composite index: WHERE on `status` + GROUP BY on `ordered_at` — eliminates scan and avoids temporary table/filesort
Composite index: JOIN on `order_id` + covers `line_total` for index-only scan — eliminates scan and avoids temporary table/filesort

queryHints.title: GROUP BY without ORDER BY (MySQL 8.4+)
queryHints.suggestion: If you need sorted output, always add an explicit ORDER BY clause.

SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM orders
WHERE payment_method = 'paypal' AND status = 'completed'
GROUP BY customer_id

            -> Index lookup on orders using idx_status (status='completed')  (cost=0.275 rows=1) (actual time=0.00746..0.00746 rows=0 loops=1)

name: Range + equality composite
query:
SELECT id, amount, paid_at
FROM payments
WHERE status = 'completed' AND amount > 200
ORDER BY paid_at DESC
explainOutput:
-> Sort: payments.paid_at DESC  (cost=1737 rows=39837) (actual time=23.3..24.3 rows=30794 loops=1)
    -> Filter: (payments.amount > 200.00)  (cost=1737 rows=39837) (actual time=0.128..18.8 rows=30794 loops=1)
        -> Index lookup on payments using idx_status (status='completed')  (cost=1737 rows=39837) (actual time=0.126..16.3 rows=50000 loops=1)

issues.title: Full table scan on `shipping_events`
issues.description: Scanning 4,286 rows. The optimizer cannot use an index to filter rows, so it reads every row in the table.

issues.title: High cost node: 100% of total

indexRecommendations.reason: Full table scan on `shipping_events` (4,286 rows) with filter on `carrier`, `event_type`

changes.explanation: Index `carrier, event_type` enables ref access instead of scanning all 4.3K rows

indexRecommendations.reason: Column `event_type` used in filter/join on `shipping_events` has no index

changes.explanation: Index `event_type` enables ref access instead of scanning all 4.3K rows

name: Revenue by country — full pipeline
query:
SELECT o.shipping_country, COUNT(DISTINCT o.id) AS orders, SUM(p.amount) AS revenue
FROM orders o
JOIN payments p ON p.order_id = o.id
WHERE p.status = 'completed'
AND o.ordered_at >= '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY o.shipping_country
ORDER BY revenue DESC
explainOutput:
-> Sort: revenue DESC  (actual time=65.8..65.8 rows=7 loops=1)
    -> Stream results  (actual time=60.2..65.8 rows=7 loops=1)
        -> Group aggregate: count(distinct orders.id), sum(payments.amount)  (actual time=60.2..65.8 rows=7 loops=1)
            -> Sort: o.shipping_country  (actual time=59.6..60.8 rows=31776 loops=1)
                -> Stream results  (cost=18335 rows=19918) (actual time=1.17..53.2 rows=31776 loops=1)
                    -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=18335 rows=19918) (actual time=1.17..50.7 rows=31776 loops=1)
                        -> Index lookup on p using idx_status (status='completed')  (cost=4392 rows=39837) (actual time=1.16..17 rows=50000 loops=1)
                        -> Filter: (o.ordered_at >= TIMESTAMP'2025-01-01 00:00:00')  (cost=0.25 rows=0.5) (actual time=566e-6..596e-6 rows=0.636 loops=50000)
                            -> Single-row index lookup on o using PRIMARY (id=p.order_id)  (cost=0.25 rows=1) (actual time=479e-6..495e-6 rows=1 loops=50000)

issues.title: Massive row estimate mismatch on `p`
issues.description: Estimated 19,918 rows but actually processed 7 (2845x fewer than estimated).

issues.title: High cost node: 100% of total
issues.description: This operation accounts for 100% of the total query cost. It is the primary bottleneck.

issues.title: Optimal unique index join on `p`
issues.description: Access type `eq_ref` — the most efficient way to retrieve joined rows.

indexRecommendations.reason: Composite index: WHERE on `status` + JOIN on `order_id` + covers `amount` for index-only scan — eliminates scan and avoids temporary table/filesort
Composite index: WHERE on `ordered_at` + GROUP BY on `shipping_country` + covers `id` for index-only scan — eliminates scan and avoids temporary table/filesort

queryHints.title: COUNT(DISTINCT ...) can be expensive
queryHints.suggestion: Consider using an approximate count (HyperLogLog via application) for analytics, or ensure the DISTINCT columns have an index.

schemaIssues.issue: `orders`.`shipping_country` is nullable but used in GROUP BY. NULL values can cause unexpected results (e.g., NOT IN with NULLs returns no rows).

name: Top products by review score
query:
SELECT p.name, p.brand, AVG(r.rating) AS avg_rating, COUNT(r.id) AS reviews
FROM products p
JOIN reviews r ON r.product_id = p.id
WHERE p.is_active = 1
GROUP BY p.id, p.name, p.brand
HAVING COUNT(r.id) >= 3
ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, reviews DESC
LIMIT 50
explainOutput:
-> Limit: 50 row(s)  (actual time=73.3..73.3 rows=50 loops=1)
    -> Sort: avg_rating DESC, reviews DESC  (actual time=73.3..73.3 rows=50 loops=1)
        -> Filter: (`count(r.id)` >= 3)  (actual time=69.6..70.8 rows=8898 loops=1)
            -> Table scan on <temporary>  (actual time=69.6..70.4 rows=9470 loops=1)
                -> Aggregate using temporary table  (actual time=69.6..69.6 rows=9470 loops=1)
                    -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=3065 rows=5931) (actual time=0.0619..41.9 rows=57043 loops=1)
                        -> Filter: (p.is_active = 1)  (cost=989 rows=965) (actual time=0.0422..2.12 rows=9500 loops=1)
                            -> Table scan on p  (cost=989 rows=9652) (actual time=0.0405..1.63 rows=10000 loops=1)
                        -> Index lookup on r using idx_product (product_id=p.id)  (cost=1.54 rows=6.14) (actual time=0.0034..0.00392 rows=6 loops=9500)

indexRecommendations.reason: Composite index: WHERE on `is_active` + GROUP BY on `name`, `brand` — eliminates scan and avoids temporary table/filesort

queryHints.title: HAVING without GROUP BY
queryHints.suggestion: Use WHERE for row-level filtering (before aggregation) and HAVING only for aggregate conditions with GROUP BY.

queryHints.title: ORDER BY + LIMIT without unique tiebreaker
queryHints.suggestion: Add a unique column (e.g., primary key) to ORDER BY for deterministic results: ORDER BY col, id.

schemaIssues.issue: `products`.`brand` is nullable but used in GROUP BY. NULL values can cause unexpected results (e.g., NOT IN with NULLs returns no rows).

name: Customer lifetime value with tiers
query:
SELECT c.tier, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS customers, AVG(sub.ltv) AS avg_ltv
FROM customers c
JOIN (
    SELECT customer_id, SUM(total_amount) AS ltv
    FROM orders
    WHERE status IN ('delivered','shipped')
    GROUP BY customer_id
) sub ON sub.customer_id = c.id
GROUP BY c.tier
explainOutput:
-> Group aggregate: count(distinct customers.id), avg(ltv)  (actual time=87.3..90.8 rows=4 loops=1)
    -> Sort: c.tier  (actual time=83.6..84.8 rows=34230 loops=1)
        -> Stream results  (cost=51403 rows=41355) (actual time=59.7..78.9 rows=34230 loops=1)
            -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=51403 rows=41355) (actual time=59.7..74.7 rows=34230 loops=1)
                -> Table scan on sub  (cost=23531..24050 rows=41355) (actual time=59.7..61.5 rows=34230 loops=1)
                    -> Materialize  (cost=23531..23531 rows=41355) (actual time=59.7..59.7 rows=34230 loops=1)
                        -> Group aggregate: sum(orders.total_amount)  (cost=19395 rows=41355) (actual time=2.88..56.7 rows=34230 loops=1)
                            -> Filter: (orders.`status` in ('delivered','shipped'))  (cost=10108 rows=92870) (actual time=2.87..51.8 rows=58332 loops=1)
                                -> Index scan on orders using idx_customer  (cost=10108 rows=99880) (actual time=2.87..42.9 rows=100000 loops=1)
                -> Single-row index lookup on c using PRIMARY (id=sub.customer_id)  (cost=0.25 rows=1) (actual time=289e-6..304e-6 rows=1 loops=34230)

issues.title: Massive row estimate mismatch on `sub`
issues.description: Estimated 41,355 rows but actually processed 4 (10339x fewer than estimated).

issues.title: High cost node: 100% of total
issues.description: This operation accounts for 100% of the total query cost. It is the primary bottleneck.

issues.title: Small table scan on `sub` (4 rows)
issues.description: Full table scan on a small table. For very small tables, a full scan can be faster than using an index.

indexRecommendations.reason: Composite index: GROUP BY on `tier` + covers `id` for index-only scan — eliminates scan and avoids temporary table/filesort

queryHints.title: Scalar subquery in SELECT — rewrite as LEFT JOIN
queryHints.suggestion: Rewrite as: SELECT t1.col, COALESCE(SUM(t2.amount), 0) FROM t1 LEFT JOIN t2 ON t2.fk = t1.id GROUP BY t1.id, t1.col. The LEFT JOIN + COALESCE ensures rows with no match show 0 instead of NULL.

queryHints.title: GROUP BY without ORDER BY (MySQL 8.4+)
queryHints.suggestion: If you need sorted output, always add an explicit ORDER BY clause.

queryHints.title: COUNT(DISTINCT ...) can be expensive
queryHints.suggestion: Consider using an approximate count (HyperLogLog via application) for analytics, or ensure the DISTINCT columns have an index.

name: Inventory health — low stock products
query:
SELECT p.id, p.name, p.stock_qty,
    COALESCE(SUM(CASE WHEN il.reason = 'purchase' THEN il.change_qty ELSE 0 END), 0) AS sold_30d
FROM products p
LEFT JOIN inventory_log il ON il.product_id = p.id
    AND il.created_at >= DATE_SUB(NOW(), INTERVAL 30 DAY)
WHERE p.is_active = 1 AND p.stock_qty < 20
GROUP BY p.id, p.name, p.stock_qty
ORDER BY sold_30d DESC
explainOutput:
-> Sort: sold_30d DESC  (actual time=6.24..6.26 rows=364 loops=1)
    -> Table scan on <temporary>  (actual time=6.17..6.19 rows=364 loops=1)
        -> Aggregate using temporary table  (actual time=6.17..6.17 rows=364 loops=1)
            -> Nested loop left join  (cost=2084 rows=3128) (actual time=0.0613..5.87 rows=462 loops=1)
                -> Filter: ((p.is_active = 1) and (p.stock_qty < 20))  (cost=989 rows=322) (actual time=0.0247..2.02 rows=364 loops=1)
                    -> Table scan on p  (cost=989 rows=9652) (actual time=0.022..1.52 rows=10000 loops=1)
                -> Filter: (il.created_at >= <cache>((now() - interval 30 day)))  (cost=2.43 rows=9.72) (actual time=0.00966..0.0104 rows=0.827 loops=364)
                    -> Index lookup on il using idx_product (product_id=p.id)  (cost=2.43 rows=9.72) (actual time=0.00888..0.00995 rows=9.98 loops=364)

issues.title: Filesort on 364 rows
issues.description: MySQL sorts 364 rows using filesort. Acceptable for small sets but may slow down as data grows.

issues.title: Temporary table with 364 rows
issues.description: A temporary table is created for 364 rows. Manageable now, but watch as data grows.

indexRecommendations.reason: Composite index: WHERE on `is_active`, `stock_qty` + GROUP BY on `name`, `stock_qty` — eliminates scan and avoids temporary table/filesort

changes.after: Index delivers rows in sorted order
changes.explanation: Index column order (`is_active` → `stock_qty` → `name`) matches WHERE + ORDER BY — MySQL skips the sort step

name: Order fulfillment time analysis
query:
SELECT o.shipping_country,
    AVG(DATEDIFF(o.delivered_at, o.ordered_at)) AS avg_delivery_days,
    MAX(DATEDIFF(o.delivered_at, o.ordered_at)) AS max_delivery_days,
    COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM orders o
WHERE o.status = 'delivered' AND o.delivered_at IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY o.shipping_country
ORDER BY avg_delivery_days DESC
explainOutput:
-> Sort: avg_delivery_days DESC  (actual time=19.5..19.5 rows=7 loops=1)
    -> Table scan on <temporary>  (actual time=19.5..19.5 rows=7 loops=1)
        -> Aggregate using temporary table  (actual time=19.5..19.5 rows=7 loops=1)
            -> Filter: (o.delivered_at is not null)  (cost=4855 rows=44946) (actual time=0.779..13.8 rows=33332 loops=1)
                -> Index lookup on o using idx_status (status='delivered')  (cost=4855 rows=49940) (actual time=0.778..12.6 rows=33332 loops=1)

issues.title: Massive row estimate mismatch on `<temporary>`
issues.description: Estimated 44,946 rows but actually processed 7 (6421x fewer than estimated).

indexRecommendations.reason: Composite index: WHERE on `status`, `delivered_at` + GROUP BY on `shipping_country` — eliminates scan and avoids temporary table/filesort

changes.after: Index delivers rows in sorted order
changes.explanation: Index column order (`status` → `delivered_at` → `shipping_country`) matches WHERE + ORDER BY — MySQL skips the sort step

schemaIssues.issue: `orders`.`shipping_country` is nullable but used in GROUP BY. NULL values can cause unexpected results (e.g., NOT IN with NULLs returns no rows).

name: Abandoned cart pattern — pending old orders
query:
SELECT o.id, c.email, o.total_amount, o.ordered_at
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.status = 'pending'
AND o.ordered_at < DATE_SUB(NOW(), INTERVAL 7 DAY)
ORDER BY o.ordered_at
LIMIT 100
explainOutput:
-> Limit: 100 row(s)  (cost=2236 rows=100) (actual time=1.42..1.59 rows=100 loops=1)
    -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=2236 rows=4167) (actual time=1.42..1.59 rows=100 loops=1)
        -> Filter: (o.`status` = 'pending')  (cost=777 rows=4167) (actual time=1.41..1.48 rows=100 loops=1)
            -> Index range scan on o using idx_ordered_at over (ordered_at < '2026-04-02 21:43:41'), with index condition: (o.ordered_at < <cache>((now() - interval 7 day)))  (cost=777 rows=49940) (actual time=1.4..1.44 rows=864 loops=1)
        -> Single-row index lookup on c using PRIMARY (id=o.customer_id)  (cost=0.25 rows=1) (actual time=933e-6..949e-6 rows=1 loops=100)

issues.title: High cost node: 100% of total
issues.description: This operation accounts for 100% of the total query cost. It is the primary bottleneck.

issues.title: Index Condition Pushdown on `o`
issues.description: ICP is active — WHERE conditions are evaluated at the storage engine level using the index, reducing row reads.

issues.title: Efficient range scan on `o`
issues.description: Index range scan using `idx_ordered_at` — only reads rows matching the range condition.

indexRecommendations.reason: Composite index: WHERE on `status`, `ordered_at` + JOIN on `customer_id` + ORDER BY on `ordered_at` — eliminates scan and avoids temporary table/filesort

queryHints.title: ORDER BY + LIMIT without unique tiebreaker
queryHints.suggestion: Add a unique column (e.g., primary key) to ORDER BY for deterministic results: ORDER BY col, id.

name: Payment reconciliation — mismatched amounts
query:
SELECT o.id AS order_id, o.total_amount, SUM(p.amount) AS paid_total,
    o.total_amount - SUM(p.amount) AS difference
FROM orders o
JOIN payments p ON p.order_id = o.id
WHERE p.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY o.id, o.total_amount
HAVING ABS(o.total_amount - SUM(p.amount)) > 0.01
explainOutput:
-> Filter: (abs((o.total_amount - `sum(p.amount)`)) > 0.01)  (actual time=67.4..73.1 rows=39302 loops=1)
    -> Table scan on <temporary>  (actual time=67.4..69.5 rows=39305 loops=1)
        -> Aggregate using temporary table  (actual time=67.4..67.4 rows=39305 loops=1)
            -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=18335 rows=39837) (actual time=1.18..47.1 rows=50000 loops=1)
                -> Index lookup on p using idx_status (status='completed')  (cost=4392 rows=39837) (actual time=1.17..17.4 rows=50000 loops=1)
                -> Single-row index lookup on o using PRIMARY (id=p.order_id)  (cost=0.25 rows=1) (actual time=493e-6..508e-6 rows=1 loops=50000)

issues.title: Temporary table with 39,302 rows
issues.description: MySQL creates a temporary table to process this operation. For large result sets, this may spill to disk.

indexRecommendations.reason: Composite index: WHERE on `status` + JOIN on `order_id` + covers `amount` for index-only scan — eliminates scan and avoids temporary table/filesort
Composite index: GROUP BY on `total_amount` — eliminates scan and avoids temporary table/filesort

queryHints.title: HAVING without GROUP BY
queryHints.suggestion: Use WHERE for row-level filtering (before aggregation) and HAVING only for aggregate conditions with GROUP BY.

queryHints.title: GROUP BY without ORDER BY (MySQL 8.4+)
queryHints.suggestion: If you need sorted output, always add an explicit ORDER BY clause.

name: YEAR() on indexed column
query: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE YEAR(ordered_at) = 2025
explainOutput:
-> Aggregate: count(0)  (cost=20096 rows=1) (actual time=11.7..11.7 rows=1 loops=1)
    -> Filter: (year(orders.ordered_at) = 2025)  (cost=10108 rows=99880) (actual time=3.52..10.2 rows=49852 loops=1)
        -> Covering index scan on orders using idx_ordered_at  (cost=10108 rows=99880) (actual time=0.0848..6.35 rows=100000 loops=1)

issues.title: Function on column in condition
issues.description: The filter "year(orders.ordered_at) = 2025" wraps a column in a function. This prevents MySQL from using any index on that column.

issues.title: High cost node: 100% of total
issues.description: This operation accounts for 100% of the total query cost. It is the primary bottleneck.

issues.title: Covering index scan on `orders`
issues.description: Full index scan but all required columns are in the index (covering). No table row reads needed.

queryHints.title: YEAR() function prevents index use — use range instead
queryHints.suggestion: Rewrite as a range: WHERE column >= '2024-01-01' AND column < '2025-01-01'. This allows an index range scan.

queryRewrites.title: Replace YEAR() with range condition
queryRewrites.original: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE YEAR(ordered_at) = 2025
queryRewrites.rewritten: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE ordered_at >= '2025-01-01' AND ordered_at < '2026-01-01'

name: DISTINCT with GROUP BY (redundant)
query:
SELECT DISTINCT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id
explainOutput: -> Group aggregate: count(0)  (cost=20096 rows=41355) (actual time=0.4..12.9 rows=43156 loops=1)\n    -> Covering index scan on orders using idx_customer  (cost=10108 rows=99880) (actual time=0.396..7.87 rows=100000 loops=1)\n

issues.title: Full index scan on `orders`
issues.description: Scanning the entire index (43,156 entries) rather than seeking to specific rows.

issues.title: High cost node: 100% of total
issues.description: This operation accounts for 100% of the total query cost. It is the primary bottleneck.

issues.title: Covering index scan on `orders`
issues.description: Full index scan but all required columns are in the index (covering). No table row reads needed.

indexRecommendations.reason: `idx_customer` reads 43.2K rows — a composite covering index (`cost`, `rows`) would be more selective and avoid table lookups
indexRecommendations.ddl:
-- Composite covering index: current index + WHERE + JOIN + aggregate columns
ALTER TABLE `orders` ADD INDEX `idx_orders_composite` (`cost`, `rows`);

changes.explanation: Index `cost, rows` enables ref access instead of scanning all 43.2K rows

changes.before: Index lookup + table data read (random I/O)
changes.explanation: All needed columns (`cost`, `rows`) are in the index — MySQL never touches the table data

indexRecommendations.reason: Column `rows` used in filter/join on `orders` has no index

changes.explanation: Index `rows` enables ref access instead of scanning all 43.2K rows

queryHints.title: GROUP BY without ORDER BY (MySQL 8.4+)
queryHints.suggestion: If you need sorted output, always add an explicit ORDER BY clause.

name: UNION vs UNION ALL
query:
SELECT id, email FROM customers WHERE tier = 'gold'
UNION
SELECT id, email FROM customers WHERE tier = 'platinum'
explainOutput:
-> Table scan on <union temporary>  (cost=12646..12960 rows=24938) (actual time=18.9..19.6 rows=12500 loops=1)
    -> Union materialize with deduplication  (cost=12646..12646 rows=24938) (actual time=18.9..18.9 rows=12500 loops=1)
        -> Filter: (customers.tier = 'gold')  (cost=5076 rows=12469) (actual time=0.0418..8.01 rows=6250 loops=1)
            -> Table scan on customers  (cost=5076 rows=49876) (actual time=0.0388..5.99 rows=50000 loops=1)
        -> Filter: (customers.tier = 'platinum')  (cost=5076 rows=12469) (actual time=0.0517..7.98 rows=6250 loops=1)
            -> Table scan on customers  (cost=5076 rows=49876) (actual time=0.0504..5.87 rows=50000 loops=1)

indexRecommendations.reason: `idx_status` reads 1,054 rows — a composite covering index (`discount_pct`, `id`, `status`) would be more selective and avoid table lookups
indexRecommendations.ddl:
-- Composite covering index: current index + WHERE + JOIN + aggregate columns
ALTER TABLE `orders` ADD INDEX `idx_orders_composite` (`discount_pct`, `id`, `status`);

changes.before: Index lookup + table data read (random I/O)
changes.explanation: All needed columns (`discount_pct`, `id`, `status`) are in the index — MySQL never touches the table data

indexRecommendations.reason: Composite index: WHERE on `discount_pct` + JOIN on `order_id` — eliminates scan and avoids temporary table/filesort

name: Deeply nested join — 5 tables
query:
SELECT c.email, o.id, oi.product_id, p.name, r.rating
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id
JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id
LEFT JOIN reviews r ON r.product_id = p.id AND r.customer_id = c.id
WHERE c.tier = 'platinum' AND o.status = 'delivered'
LIMIT 50
explainOutput:
-> Limit: 50 row(s)  (cost=66261 rows=50) (actual time=1.74..2.25 rows=50 loops=1)
    -> Nested loop left join  (cost=66261 rows=56733) (actual time=1.74..2.24 rows=50 loops=1)
        -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=46404 rows=33672) (actual time=1.72..2.06 rows=50 loops=1)
            -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=34619 rows=33672) (actual time=1.71..1.99 rows=50 loops=1)
                -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=22834 rows=12485) (actual time=1.69..1.87 rows=24 loops=1)
                    -> Index lookup on o using idx_status (status='delivered')  (cost=5355 rows=49940) (actual time=1.67..1.68 rows=178 loops=1)
                    -> Filter: (c.tier = 'platinum')  (cost=0.25 rows=0.25) (actual time=0.00101..0.00102 rows=0.135 loops=178)
                        -> Single-row index lookup on c using PRIMARY (id=o.customer_id)  (cost=0.25 rows=1) (actual time=877e-6..893e-6 rows=1 loops=178)
                -> Index lookup on oi using idx_order (order_id=o.id)  (cost=0.674 rows=2.7) (actual time=0.00466..0.00489 rows=2.08 loops=24)
            -> Single-row index lookup on p using PRIMARY (id=oi.product_id)  (cost=0.25 rows=1) (actual time=0.0011..0.00112 rows=1 loops=50)
        -> Filter: (r.product_id = oi.product_id)  (cost=0.421 rows=1.68) (actual time=0.00364..0.00364 rows=0 loops=50)
            -> Index lookup on r using idx_customer (customer_id=o.customer_id)  (cost=0.421 rows=1.68) (actual time=0.00334..0.00351 rows=1.16 loops=50)

name: Self-referencing category tree
query:
SELECT c.name AS category, p.name AS parent
FROM categories c
LEFT JOIN categories p ON p.id = c.parent_id
WHERE c.parent_id IS NOT NULL
explainOutput:
-> Nested loop left join  (cost=83.2 rows=180) (actual time=0.032..0.208 rows=180 loops=1)
    -> Filter: (c.parent_id is not null)  (cost=20.2 rows=180) (actual time=0.0175..0.0443 rows=180 loops=1)
        -> Table scan on c  (cost=20.2 rows=200) (actual time=0.0147..0.0369 rows=200 loops=1)
    -> Single-row index lookup on p using PRIMARY (id=c.parent_id)  (cost=0.251 rows=1) (actual time=804e-6..819e-6 rows=1 loops=180)

name: Cross-table aggregation with CASE
query:
SELECT p.brand,
    SUM(CASE WHEN r.rating >= 4 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS positive,
    SUM(CASE WHEN r.rating <= 2 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS negative,
    COUNT(*) AS total
FROM products p
JOIN reviews r ON r.product_id = p.id
WHERE p.is_active = 1
GROUP BY p.brand
ORDER BY total DESC
explainOutput:
-> Sort: total DESC  (actual time=57.9..57.9 rows=12 loops=1)
    -> Table scan on <temporary>  (actual time=57.9..57.9 rows=12 loops=1)
        -> Aggregate using temporary table  (actual time=57.9..57.9 rows=12 loops=1)
            -> Nested loop inner join  (cost=3065 rows=5931) (actual time=0.0455..39.5 rows=57043 loops=1)
                -> Filter: (p.is_active = 1)  (cost=989 rows=965) (actual time=0.0283..1.88 rows=9500 loops=1)
                    -> Table scan on p  (cost=989 rows=9652) (actual time=0.0269..1.39 rows=10000 loops=1)
                -> Index lookup on r using idx_product (product_id=p.id)  (cost=1.54 rows=6.14) (actual time=0.00318..0.0037 rows=6 loops=9500)
